published_date: 2026-05-05
video_id: kK5ckiotffM
url: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kK5ckiotffM
title: The Macro Minute: Are we nearing the end of US exceptionalism in asset markets?

Happy Tuesday out there, team 42. It's
your skipper here, Darius Dale, to
present our macro minute for Tuesday,
May 5th, 2026. As always, we'll start
with the executive summary from today's
Lead-Off Morning Note. So, let's dive
right in. Today's key macro question is,
are we nearing the end of US
exceptionalism in asset markets? Our
answer is not likely, as long as the
Strait of Hormuz remains effectively
closed, because the US remains best
positioned to weather global supply
chain disruptions.
Best positioned does not equal well
positioned, as evidenced by this
morning's data. Most notably, the April
ISM services PMI data supported the
consensus outlook for slowing growth and
did not support the consensus outlook
for slowing inflation.
Moreover, the weak positive impulse in
private sector job openings and strong
negative impulse in the civilian labor
civilian labor force combined to suggest
a marginally tighter labor market that
should make the Fed cautious regarding
further easing. The Fed is likely on
hold for the foreseeable future.
Transitioning to our 42 macro dashboard,
as always, we'll wrap up with a question
from our community. This one's titled
RPM, in relation to gold price. I think
they mean RMP. It says, "Hello, DD.
Thanks for sharing the macro scouting
report. Always a pleasure to hear your
research and incredible knowledge across
disciplines." Thank you for the kind
compliment. The question reads, "I'd
like to ask you ask if you may comment a
little bit further on how war risk to
Fed reserve management purchases may
have affected gold prices back in
January, like you've mentioned, or any
other implication it may have moving
forward." Apologies if my question is
too broad, but I'm generally curious on
how the reserve management purchases
dynamic can have an impact on the
overall liquidity and its implications
in general price discovery or cushion.
Thanks in advance and best regards. So,
excellent question.
So, from my vantage point, I think the
Kevin Warsh discussion definitely has a
big influence on the gold price. Recall
that gold suffered its I think its
largest down day in like 50 years, going
back to January 30th, when the
when it was announced that Kevin Warsh
received the nomination for Fed chair
from the Trump administration. And so,
the very clear implication there is that
there's a
a risk out there in the in the in the
global financial markets that Kevin
Warsh's very hawkish views on the Fed
balance sheet, and thinks the balance
sheet is, quote, "Trillions larger than
it needs to be." We've been very
consistent in our research
about, you know, the the likelihood
that, hey, if they are going to pursue a
meaningfully smaller balance sheet and a
much less responsive Fed from the
perspective of its value using its
balance sheet in response to, you know,
volatility in financial markets, then
you're ultimately going to have to use
the commercial banking sector's asset
base and leverage, and ultimately change
the banking regulation, specifically
in a way that would allow treasuries to
be used as high-quality liquid assets
instead of just bank reserves.
You have to change those bank
regulations regulations that allows
you know, this sort of, you know, unwind
of the Fed's balance sheet to be kind of
relatively well digested by the economy
and financial markets. So, that's number
one. That that that's that was kind of
what kicked off the correction we've
seen in gold. I would say number two,
you obviously have the liquidity issues
being born out of the embargoed
petroleum products and fertilizer input
products from the Strait of Hormuz and
the broader Middle Eastern region,
because ultimately, those economies
aren't receiving the export revenue that
they need to,
you know, generate the the the profits
and savings that they that they would
ultimately use from a
liquidity and the dollar recycling
standpoint. For example,
this morning it was reported that the
Saudi Arabia's budget balance more than
doubled on a year-over-year basis, with
a big chunk of that doubling coming in
the most recent quarter, as a function
of its inability to sell enough crude
oil. And so, that's obviously a big hit,
because you have said Saudi Arabia's net
their net savings rate is declining,
which ultimately means that their net
international investment surplus, the
growth rate of that net international
investment surplus, is declining. And
this is exactly the hit to the liquidity
cycle that we've been warning about from
the perspective of the closure of the
Strait of Hormuz. And then finally, one
the final thing that I think is weighing
on the gold price is the fact that
you've seen a a very hawkish shift in
forward rate pricing across major
central bank curves from the start of
the US-Israel-Iran conflict to now. So,
if you look at what's been priced into
one-year overnight index swaps for the
Bank of Japan and Swiss National Bank,
effectively one to two rate hikes have
been priced in at the margins for those
central banks. Three to four rate hikes
have been priced in at the margins in
terms of the movement in one-year IS
relative to the policy rate for both the
Fed and the ECB. And then you also have
four to five rate hikes priced into the
Bank of England's short rates of curve
through through one-year IS. So, again,
Fed balance sheet risk, the closure of
the Strait of Hormuz and the ultimate
risk that that poses to the global
liquidity cycle, plus the directionally
hawkish pricing across the forward rate
curves for all these major central
banks, are three factors that have
combined to weigh on the gold price. So,
if you want a durable rally in gold from
here, you're going to have to start
unwinding those dynamics at the margins.
And the biggest thing that can cause an
unwind is the opening of the Strait of
Hormuz there. So, we'll wrap up there.
Darius Dale here, presenting our macro
macro minute for Tuesday, May 5th, 2026.
Best of luck out there today. We'll
catch back here tomorrow. Cheers.
